questions.text: The most commonly used body fluid by most of the higher organisms for circulation is:
questions.explanation: Blood is the primary fluid for transport of nutrients, gases, and waste in higher organisms.

questions.text: The percentage of plasma in total blood volume is approximately:
questions.explanation: Plasma constitutes nearly 55 percent of the blood, while the formed elements constitute the remaining 45 percent.

questions.text: Which plasma protein is primarily involved in the defense mechanisms of the body?
questions.explanation: Globulins are primarily involved in defense mechanisms (antibodies), while fibrinogen is for clotting and albumins for osmotic balance.

questions.text: Plasma without the clotting factors is called:
questions.explanation: Serum is the liquid part of blood remaining after the clotting factors have been removed.

questions.text: The average lifespan of a human Red Blood Cell (RBC) is:
questions.explanation: RBCs have an average lifespan of 120 days, after which they are destroyed in the spleen.

questions.text: The 'Graveyard of RBCs' is the:
questions.explanation: Old and damaged RBCs are filtered out and destroyed in the spleen.

questions.text: Which of the following are the most abundant cells in total White Blood Cells (WBCs)?
questions.explanation: Neutrophils are the most numerous (60-65%), whereas basophils are the least (0.5-1%).

questions.text: Which WBCs secrete histamine, serotonin, and heparin and are involved in inflammatory reactions?
questions.explanation: Basophils secrete these chemicals and play a major role in inflammation.

questions.text: Universal donor and universal recipient blood groups are ________ and ________ respectively.
questions.explanation: Blood group O (specifically O negative) is the universal donor, while AB (specifically AB positive) is the universal recipient.

questions.text: Erythroblastosis foetalis can occur when:
options: Mother is Rh +ve, Fetus is Rh -ve
Mother is Rh -ve, Fetus is Rh +ve
questions.explanation: This condition occurs in a second Rh+ pregnancy of an Rh- mother due to maternal antibodies attacking fetal RBCs.

questions.text: During the process of blood clotting, prothrombin is converted to thrombin by the enzyme complex:
questions.explanation: Thrombokinase (or prothrombinase) converts inactive prothrombin into active thrombin.

questions.text: Which mineral ion plays a very important role in blood clotting?
questions.explanation: Calcium ions (Ca++) are necessary for almost every step of the coagulation cascade.

questions.text: Which of the following describes the human heart?
questions.explanation: The human heart is myogenic, meaning the impulse for contraction originates within the muscle itself (SA node).

questions.text: The wall of the left ventricle is thicker than the right ventricle because:
options: It has to pump blood to the entire body
questions.explanation: The left ventricle requires greater pressure to pump blood through the systemic circulation compared to the pulmonary circulation.

questions.text: The pacemaker of the heart is the:
questions.explanation: The Sino-atrial Node (SA node) generates the action potential that initiates the heartbeat.

questions.text: In a standard ECG, the P-wave represents:
questions.explanation: The P-wave indicates the electrical excitation (contraction) of the atria.

questions.text: The QRS complex in an ECG represents:
questions.explanation: The QRS complex corresponds to the contraction of the ventricles.

questions.text: Cardiac output is defined as:
questions.explanation: Cardiac output is the volume of blood pumped by each ventricle per minute (approx. 5 Liters).

questions.text: Double circulation consists of:
questions.explanation: Double circulation involves two pathways: pulmonary (to lungs) and systemic (to rest of body).

questions.text: The hepatic portal system carries blood from ________ to ________.
questions.explanation: The hepatic portal vein carries blood from the intestine to the liver before it goes to the systemic circulation.

questions.text: Which blood vessel has valves to prevent backflow of blood?
questions.explanation: Veins operate under low pressure and have valves to ensure one-way flow towards the heart.

questions.text: The lub-dub sounds of the heart are produced by:
questions.explanation: Lub is the closure of AV valves; Dub is the closure of semilunar valves.

questions.text: A standard blood pressure reading is 120/80 mmHg. 120 represents:
questions.explanation: Systolic pressure is the higher pressure in the arteries during ventricular contraction.

questions.text: What is the result of the blockage of coronary arteries?
questions.explanation: Coronary Artery Disease (CAD) involves narrowing of the arteries that supply blood to the heart muscle.

questions.text: Which heart condition is characterized by acute chest pain when not enough oxygen reaches the heart muscle?
questions.explanation: Angina is a symptom of reduced blood flow to the heart.

questions.text: The middle layer of the heart wall is the:
questions.explanation: The myocardium consists of cardiac muscle tissue responsible for pumping.

questions.text: Lymph differs from blood in that it lacks:
questions.explanation: Lymph is a colorless fluid containing specialized lymphocytes but lacking RBCs and large proteins.

questions.text: The tricuspid valve is located between:
questions.explanation: The tricuspid valve guards the opening between the right atrium and right ventricle.

questions.text: Chordae tendineae are found in:
questions.explanation: These are 'heart strings' that prevent the AV valves from flipping into the atria during ventricular contraction.

questions.text: The volume of blood pumped out by each ventricle during a cardiac cycle is about 70 mL. This is the:
questions.explanation: Stroke volume is the amount of blood ejected by one ventricle in one contraction.

questions.text: Which WBC is phagocytic in nature?
questions.explanation: Neutrophils and monocytes are phagocytic cells which destroy foreign organisms.

questions.text: During joint diastole:
options: Both atria and ventricles are relaxed
questions.explanation: In joint diastole, all four chambers are in a relaxed state.

questions.text: The neural center that can moderate cardiac function through the ANS is located in the:
questions.explanation: The medulla oblongata contains the cardiovascular center.

questions.text: Sympathetic neural signals ________ the rate of heartbeat.
questions.explanation: Sympathetic stimulation increases heart rate and strength of contraction (fight or flight).

questions.text: Which blood vessel carries oxygenated blood from the lungs to the heart?
questions.explanation: Pulmonary veins are the only veins in the adult body that carry oxygenated blood.

questions.text: T-wave in ECG represents 'return to normal state'. This is called:
questions.explanation: Repolarization is the recovery of the resting membrane potential.

questions.text: High blood pressure (Hypertension) is defined as a persistent reading of:
questions.explanation: Consistent readings above 140/90 are diagnosed as hypertension.

questions.text: Inter-ventricular septum is:
questions.explanation: The inter-ventricular septum is a thick muscular wall separating the two ventricles.

questions.text: Bicuspid (Mitral) valve is found between:
questions.explanation: The bicuspid valve prevents backflow into the left atrium.

questions.text: Which component of blood is responsible for producing antibodies?
questions.explanation: B-lymphocytes produce antibodies to provide immunity.
